children.value: You do not need to install anything. The Python cells on this page run
: move a slider and every result that depends on it recomputes.
A few lines of Python replace a pocket calculator, a formula sheet, and a unit-conversion table. This page walks through the four skills you will reuse all semester:

children.html: 6.626×10−346.626 \times 10^{-34}6.626×10−34

children.value: so a formula is typed once and reused forever
to see physics that tables of numbers hide
Everything here uses the physics of this chapter: photons, the hydrogen spectrum, and blackbody radiation.
Five lines set up the entire toolkit. The star is
, which knows every physical constant so you never mistype one (
A green laser pointer emits 532 nm light. What is the energy of one of its photons? Type the formula

children.html: E=hc/λE = h c / \lambdaE=hc/λ

children.value: almost exactly as you would write it:

model.html: 3.733920784114527e-19

children.value: Joules are inconvenient at the atomic scale, so chemists quote photon energies in
(multiply by Avogadro’s number). Unit conversion is just arithmetic:

model.html:
<marimo-island
    data-app-id="jb-21f7d97d5e42"
    data-cell-idx="5"
    data-reactive="true"
>
    <marimo-cell-output>
    <marimo-mime-renderer data-mime='"text/markdown"' data-data='"\u003cspan class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"\u003e\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003eone green photon carries \u003cstrong\u003e3.734e-19 J\u003c/strong\u003e, i.e. \u003cstrong\u003e2.33 eV\u003c/strong\u003e, and a mole of them delivers \u003cstrong\u003e225 kJ/mol\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/span\u003e"'></marimo-mime-renderer>
    </marimo-cell-output>

</marimo-island>

children.value: A mole of green photons carries about 225 kJ, and a typical C-C single bond costs 347 kJ/mol. Can light break a bond? Drag the slider and find out. Making an interactive control is one line of code:

model.html:
<marimo-island
    data-app-id="jb-21f7d97d5e42"
    data-cell-idx="6"
    data-reactive="true"
>
    <marimo-cell-output>
    <marimo-ui-element object-id='jb21f7d97d5e42Xref-0' random-id='7d682df6-ba74-5718-c7fd-66691d5287e5'><marimo-slider data-initial-value='532' data-label='"\u003cspan class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"\u003e\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003ephoton wavelength (nm)\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/span\u003e"' data-start='200' data-stop='1000' data-step='2' data-steps='[]' data-debounce='false' data-disabled='false' data-orientation='"horizontal"' data-show-value='true' data-include-input='false' data-full-width='false'></marimo-slider></marimo-ui-element>
    </marimo-cell-output>
    <marimo-ui-element object-id='517225be-4718-af3d-000d-d2cb52e698b3' random-id='517225be-4718-af3d-000d-d2cb52e698b3'><marimo-code-editor data-initial-value='"lam_pick = mo.ui.slider(\n    start=200, stop=1000, step=2, value=532,\n    show_value=True, label=\"photon wavelength (nm)\",\n)\nlam_pick"' data-label='null' data-language='"python"' data-placeholder='""' data-disabled='false' data-show-copy-button='true' data-debounce='false'></marimo-code-editor></marimo-ui-element>
</marimo-island>

A 532 nm photon (visible) carries 2.33 eV, or 225 kJ/mol per mole of photons: no, too weak for a C-C bond (347 kJ/mol).

children.value: Notice the chemistry hiding in this toy: visible light cannot touch a C-C bond, but ultraviolet light below roughly 345 nm can. That is why UV radiation damages molecules and sunscreen exists.
The de Broglie wavelength, in three lines

children.label: The de Broglie wavelength, in three lines

children.value: The same calculator settles a chapter question: why do electrons diffract but baseballs do not? Compute

children.html: λ=h/mv\lambda = h / mvλ=h/mv

children.value: for both. For the electron we use its speed in the ground state of the Bohr model

children.html: v≈2.19×106v \approx 2.19 \times 10^6v≈2.19×106

children.value: Gray arrows mean the photon lies outside the visible window. Only the Balmer series lands in it, which is why hydrogen discharge tubes glow red-pink: you are looking mostly at the 656 nm line you computed above.
started quantum mechanics. Typed as code, it is three lines:

children.value: The default 5800 K is the surface of the Sun: its peak sits right in the visible band (evolution is not a coincidence). Cool the slider to 3000 K, a light bulb filament, and watch the peak slide into the infrared, which is why incandescent bulbs waste most of their energy as heat.
Symbolic math: let the computer do calculus

children.label: Symbolic math: let the computer do calculus

children.html: λmaxT=b\lambda_{max} T = bλmax​T=b

children.value: . In 1900 deriving it took real work;
differentiates Planck’s law and finds the root for you. One standard physics trick first: substitute the dimensionless variable

children.html: x=hc/λkBTx = hc / \lambda k_B Tx=hc/λkB​T

children.value: , so Planck’s law becomes a clean scale-free shape

children.html: x5/(ex−1)x^5 / (e^x - 1)x5/(ex−1)

children.value: and every temperature collapses onto one curve:

model.html: ||[ W\left(- \frac{5}{e^{5}}\right) + 5 ||]

children.value: That output is not a decimal:
, in terms of the Lambert W function. Turned into numbers, it hands us Wien’s constant:

model.html:
<marimo-island
    data-app-id="jb-21f7d97d5e42"
    data-cell-idx="18"
    data-reactive="true"
>
    <marimo-cell-output>
    <marimo-mime-renderer data-mime='"text/markdown"' data-data='"\u003cspan class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"\u003e\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003enumerically \u003cmarimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\"\u003e||(x^* = 4.9651||)\u003c/marimo-tex\u003e, so the derived Wien constant is \u003cmarimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\"\u003e||(b = hc / x^* k_B||)\u003c/marimo-tex\u003e = \u003cstrong\u003e2.8978e-03 m K\u003c/strong\u003e (scipy tabulates 2.8978e-03), which puts the 5800 K peak at \u003cstrong\u003e500 nm\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/span\u003e"'></marimo-mime-renderer>
    </marimo-cell-output>
    <marimo-cell-code hidden>wien_b%20%3D%20const.h%20*%20const.c%20%2F%20(const.k%20*%20float(x_star))%0A%0Amo.md(%0A%20%20%20%20f%22numerically%20%24x%5E*%20%3D%20%7Bfloat(x_star)%3A.4f%7D%24%2C%20so%20the%20derived%20Wien%20constant%20is%20%22%0A%20%20%20%20f%22%24b%20%3D%20hc%20%2F%20x%5E*%20k_B%24%20%3D%20**%7Bwien_b%3A.4e%7D%20m%20K**%20(scipy%20tabulates%20%7Bconst.Wien%3A.4e%7D)%2C%20%22%0A%20%20%20%20f%22which%20puts%20the%205800%20K%20peak%20at%20**%7Bwien_b%20%2F%205800%20*%201e9%3A.0f%7D%20nm**%22%0A)</marimo-cell-code>
</marimo-island>

children.value: The result matches the dashed line in the plot above and scipy’s tabulated constant to four digits. You will use the same maximize-by-differentiating trick later to locate maxima of probability densities.
Quantum wavefunctions are complex-valued, so Python treats complex numbers as first-class citizens (

children.html: z=1+3 iz = 1 + \sqrt{3}\,iz=1+3​i

<marimo-island
    data-app-id="jb-21f7d97d5e42"
    data-cell-idx="20"
    data-reactive="true"
>
    <marimo-cell-output>
    <marimo-mime-renderer data-mime='"text/markdown"' data-data='"\u003cspan class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"\u003e\u003cspan class=\"paragraph\"\u003e\u003cmarimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\"\u003e||(z = 1 + 1.732\\,i||)\u003c/marimo-tex\u003e, with conjugate \u003cmarimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\"\u003e||(z^* = 1 - 1.732\\,i||)\u003c/marimo-tex\u003e, modulus \u003cmarimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\"\u003e||(|z| = \\sqrt{z z^*} = 2.000||)\u003c/marimo-tex\u003e, and polar form \u003cmarimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\"\u003e||(r = 2.000||)\u003c/marimo-tex\u003e, \u003cmarimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\"\u003e||(\\varphi = 0.333\\,\\pi||)\u003c/marimo-tex\u003e\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/span\u003e"'></marimo-mime-renderer>

The first time you move a control, a Python runtime (Pyodide, Python compiled to WebAssembly) loads in the background with NumPy, SciPy, Matplotlib, and SymPy, and from then on every cell reruns locally in your browser.
